coredata.dc:title: Synthesis, surface modification and biological imaging of aggregation-induced emission (AIE) dye doped silica nanoparticles
coredata.dc:creator: Mao, Liucheng
Liu, Meiying
Xu, Dazhuang
Wan, Qing
Huang, Qiang
Jiang, Ruming
Shi, Yingge
Deng, Fengjie
Zhang, Xiaoyong
Wei, Yen
coredata.dc:description: Fluorescent silica nanoparticles (FSNPs) have been extensively investigated for various biomedical applications in recently years. However, the aggregation of organic dyes in silica nanoparticles also leads the significant fluorescence quenching owing to the aggregation caused quenching effects of organic dyes. Herein, we developed a rather facile strategy to fabricate FSNPs with desirable fluorescent properties through non-covalent incorporation of fluorophores with aggregation-induced emission (AIE) feature into silica nanoparticles, which were subsequently modified with functional polymers. The resultant FSNPs polymer nanocomposites (named as FSNPs-poly(IA-co-PEGMA)) exhibited uniform spherical morphology, high water dispersiity, and bright red fluorescence. Cytotoxicity results indicate that FSNPs-poly(IA-co-PEGMA) possess excellent biocompatibility. Cell uptake behavior suggests FSNPs-poly(IA-co-PEGMA) are of great potential for biological imaging applications. Taken together, we have reported a facile method for the fabrication of FSNPs through non-covalent encapsulation using an AIE-active dye. These FSNPs can be further functionalized with functional polymers through ring-opening reaction and the resultant FSNPs-poly(IA-co-PEGMA) showed great potential for biological imaging. More importantly, we believe that many other functional components could also be integrated into these FSNPs through the facile ring-opening reaction. Therefore, this method should be a facile and general tool for fabrication of polymer functionalized AIE-active FSNPs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Aggregation-induced emission
Silica nanoparticles
Surface modification
Biological imaging

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast footnotes grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst pubtype ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis of FSNPs-poly(IA-co-PEGMA), (1) the AIE dye (ATPHE) encapsulated in the core of silica nanoparticles and surface modification of FSNPs with amino groups (FSNPs-NH2), (2) the preparation of the poly(IA-co-PEGMA) via living free radical polymerization, (3) the fabrication of the polymers-coating FSNPs surface modified with the PEG-containing polymers through the ring-opening polymerization.
1H NMR spectra of poly(IA-co-PEGMA) and FSNPs-poly(IA-co-PEGMA).
TEM images of (A) FSNPs, scale bar=20nm. (B) FSNPs-poly(IA-co-PEGMA), scale bar=500nm.
FT-IR spectra of FSNPs, FSNPs-NH2 and FSNPs-poly(IA-co-PEGMA).
XPS spectra of FSNPs, FSNPs-NH2 and FSNPs-poly(IA-co-PEGMA). (A) Si2p region, (B) C1s region, (C) N1s region and (D) O1s region.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of FSNPs-poly(IA-co-PEGMA) in distilled water. The inset images is the photographys of FSNPs-poly(IA-co-PEGMA) water suspensions; left (under natural light) and right (under UV lamp irradiation at 365nm).
Cell viability values estimated by CCK-8 assay using different concentrations of FSNPs-poly(IA-co-PEGMA). HeLa cells were incubated with FSNPs-poly(IA-co-PEGMA) for 12 and 24h.

CLSM images of HeLa cells incubated with 40μgmL−1 of FSNPs-poly(IA-co-PEGMA) for 3h. (A) Fluorescent images were obtained by exciting with 488nm laser, (B) Bright field, (C) merged image. Scale bar=20μm.
Element contents of FSNPs, FSNPs-NH2 and FSNPs-poly(IA-co-PEGMA) based on XPS analysis.

head.ce:title: Synthesis, surface modification and biological imaging of aggregation-induced emission (AIE) dye doped silica nanoparticles

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Nanchang University, 999 Xuefu Avenue, Nanchang 330031, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry and the Tsinghua Center for Frontier Polymer Research, Tsinghua University, Beijing, 100084, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and the Tsinghua Center for Frontier Polymer Research

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluorescent silica nanoparticles with intensive luminescence, high water solubility and good biocompatibility were fabricated via direct encapsulation of aggregation-induced emission dye and subsequently surface modification of polymers through ring-opening reaction.

Fluorescent silica nanoparticles (FSNPs) have been extensively investigated for various biomedical applications in recently years. However, the aggregation of organic dyes in silica nanoparticles also leads the significant fluorescence quenching owing to the aggregation caused quenching effects of organic dyes. Herein, we developed a rather facile strategy to fabricate FSNPs with desirable fluorescent properties through non-covalent incorporation of fluorophores with aggregation-induced emission (AIE) feature into silica nanoparticles, which were subsequently modified with functional polymers. The resultant FSNPs polymer nanocomposites (named as FSNPs-poly(IA-co-PEGMA)) exhibited uniform spherical morphology, high water dispersiity, and bright red fluorescence. Cytotoxicity results indicate that FSNPs-poly(IA-co-PEGMA) possess excellent biocompatibility. Cell uptake behavior suggests FSNPs-poly(IA-co-PEGMA) are of great potential for biological imaging applications. Taken together, we have reported a facile method for the fabrication of FSNPs through non-covalent encapsulation using an AIE-active dye. These FSNPs can be further functionalized with functional polymers through ring-opening reaction and the resultant FSNPs-poly(IA-co-PEGMA) showed great potential for biological imaging. More importantly, we believe that many other functional components could also be integrated into these FSNPs through the facile ring-opening reaction. Therefore, this method should be a facile and general tool for fabrication of polymer functionalized AIE-active FSNPs.

ce:section.ce:para: Various inorganic nanomaterials, including Au nanoparticles [1], hydroxyapatite (HAp) [2], silica nanoparticles [3] or mesoporous silica nanoparticles (MSNs) [4,5], play a pivotal role in the field of cell imaging and drug delivery. Owing to their unique physical and chemical properties, these materials could also be integrated with various fluorescent dyes or anti-cancer drugs for broadening their biological applications [6–17]. Among these hybrid systems, fluorescent silica nanoparticles (FSNPs) with quite fascinating properties have been extensively developed for biomedical applications. As compared with other fluorescent inorganic nanoparticles (FINs), FSNPs exhibited numerous advantages such as good optical transparency, controllable particle size, ease of surface functionalization and good cellular membrance-penetrating capacity [18,19]. Such superior properties enable FSNPs better candidates for biomedical applications. However, FSNPs based on conventional organic dyes such as fluorescein and rhodamine etc have some limitations. It is noteworthy that these fluorescent dyes suffer from the obvious emission quenching, which is partially or completely because they experience intense intermolecular π-π stacking interactions [20,21]. This phenomenon is known as aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ), which make the great challenge for fabrication of fluorescent nanoprobes with desirable optical properties because the aggregation of organic dyes is normally occurred in biological systems.

On the contrary, another type of fluorescent dyes with aggregation-induced emission (AIE) characteristics have attracted much research interest for fabrication of luminescent nanomaterials since the concept was coined by Tang group in 2001 [22–24]. Compared with the conventional organic dyes, AIE-active dyes are weak fluorescence or non-fluorescence in dilute solutions whereas emit strong fluorescence in concentrated solutions or solids [25]. The mechanism of AIE phenomenon is interpreted as restriction of intramolecular rotations (RIR) [26]. Thus it can be seen that AIE-active dyes are most suitable for fabrication multifunctional luminescent nanomaterials for biomedical applications (e.g. biological imaging or targeted therapy) [25,27–41]. Incorporation of AIE-active dyes into silica nanoparticles through both covalent and non-covalent methods have demonstrated to form AIE dyes doped fluorescent silica nanoparticles [42–44]. However, the covalent methods usually require complicated experimental procedures and long reaction time to conjugate AIE-active dyes with silica precursors. These AIE-active dyes containing silica precursors can be further used for fabrication of AIE-active dyes doped silica nanoparticles. Other than the covalent method, non-covalent encapsulation of AIE-active dyes into silica matrix for doping silica nanoparticles provide a new opportunity [45–49]. This physical coating of AIE-active dyes is simple and effective compared to covalent methods. On the other hand, encapsulation of AIE-active dyes by other inorganic materials such as hydroxyapatite, layered double hydroxides and metal organic frameworks have also demonstrated by some groups [50–56]. However, to the best of our knowledge, the surface modification of these AIE-active dyes doped inorganic nanocomposites has not been investigated thus far.

Surface modification of silica nanoparticles with silicate derivatives such as amine and vinyl for grafting of specific target molecules or non-specific hydrophilic polymers is important to enhance their biocompatibility and physicochemical stability. In recent years, the surface of silica nanoparticles covering suitable a polyethylene glycol (PEG)-containing polymer attracted great attention owing to the good hydrophilicity and excellent biocompatibility of PEG [57]. In addition, due to the versatile end group chemistry of the polymers coated silica nanoparticles, most anti-cancer drugs such as cis-platinum and folic acid could be introduced to open up a wide of promising applications. Combined the fluorescent signal of the AIE dyes doped FSNPs with the biological activity of the PEG-containing polymers make multifunctional FSNPs become promising candidates for the integration of diagnosis and treatment.

In this contribution, we successfully design and fabricate core-shell FSNPs with fluorescent molecules as the core and hydrophilic polymers as the shell. As shown in Scheme 1 , the AIE-active dye 10-cetyl-10H-phenothiazine-3,7-(4,40-aminophenyl) acetonitrile (ATPHE) as a contrast agent was first encapsulated in silica core via modified Stöber method. Then as-prepared AIE dyes doped FSNPs were modified with 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES) to introduce amino groups for further surface modification. Finally, the functional polymers with anhydride group and PEG were immobilized on FSNPs through the ring-opening reaction. Due to the introduction of abundant carboxyl group and hydrophilic polymers, the functionalized FSNPs showed great water dispersity and functional groups for potential drug loading and delivery.

Tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS), 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES), itaconic anhydride (IA), poly(ethylene glycol) methyl ether methacrylate (PEGMA, Mw: 950Da, 98%), 2,2-azodiisobutyronitrile (AIBN) were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Anhydrous toluene was obtained from Heowns (Tianjin, China). All other solvents were purchased from commercial sources and used directly without further purification. Deionized water was used in all experiments. The AIE dye, phenothiazine (named as ATPHE) containing two amino groups, was prepared according to our previous report [58].

The fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) spectra were recorded on a Nicolet5700 (Thermo Nicolet corporation) using KBr pellets. In order to determine the element composition, the final products were characterized by X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS), which was supplied from a VGESCALAB 220-IXL spectrometer. The morphology and size of the silica nanoparticles was measured using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) on a Hitachi 7650B microscope operated at 80 Kv, and the TEM specimens were made by placing a drop of the nanoparticles suspension on a carbon-coated copper grid. The size distribution of FSNPs-poly(IA-co-PEGMA) in water was determined by dynamic laser scattering (DLS) based on a zeta Plus apparatus (ZetaPlus, Brookhaven Instruments, Holtsville, NY). Fluorescence spectra were measured on the fluorescence spectrophotometer (FSP, model: C11367-11, Japan).

The AIE-active dye (named as ATPHE) was synthesized according to our previous report and its chemical structure was listed in Fig. S1 [58]. The obtained AIE dye (ATPHE) was encapsulated in silica NPs through a non-covalent embedment approach. First, ATPHE (0.1mmol, 50mg) was dissolved in 5mL THF. Then 1mL of the above solution was added to 16mL ethanol, and 1.2mL ammonium hydroxide was added. The reaction mixture was stirred at room temperature for 30min. Subsequently, 0.5mL TEOS and 1.5mL ethanol were mixed and the mixture was added dropwise to the above reaction mixture, and then stirred for 20h at room temperature. Finally, the resultant AIE dye containing silica nanoparticles were obtained by centrifugation (6000rpm for 5min) and were respectively washed three times with toluene and deionized water. The resulting products were dried in vacuum drying oven at 40°C.

The amine modified FSNPs were prepared via silane coupling reaction between the hydroxyl groups of the FSNPs and APTES. In brief, 200mg FSNPs was dispersed in the solution with 60mL ethanol and 20mL water. Subsequently, 1.6mL APTES was added to the above solution and stirred for 24h at room temperature. The resulting amine modified FSNPs were separated by centrifugation (6000rpm for 3min) and were washed three times with ethanol and followed by dried in vacuum drying oven at 40°C.

The poly(IA-co-PEGMA) copolymers were synthesized via living free radical polymerization. In a typical procedure, PEGMA (1.8g, 2mmol), IA (450mg, 4mmol), AIBN (8mg) and distilled dry THF (5mL) were added in a 25mL Schlenk tube. The tube was evacuated under vacuum and filled with dry N2 three times prior to transfer to a preheated oil bath maintained at 70°C for 10h. After that, the poly(IA-co-PEGMA) was obtained via precipitation from THF to diethyl ether for three times. Finally, white precipitate was collected by filtration and drying.

Preparation of FSNPs-poly(IA-co-PEGMA) was via ring-open reaction between the amine groups on the surface of the FSNPs-NH2 and anhydride of poly(IA-co-PEGMA). The experimental procedure could be described as follows: poly(IA-co-PEGMA) (100mg) and FSNPs-NH2 (50mg) were dispersed in anhydrous toluene. The mixture was stirred at 40°C for 6h. The synthesized FSNPs-poly(IA-co-PEGMA) were collected by centrifugation at 6000rpm for 3min and were washed at least three times with ethanol. The final products were dried in vacuum drying oven at 40°C.

In order to evaluate the cytotoxicity of FSNPs-poly(IA-co-PEGMA), cytotoxicity assay was performed using cell counting kit-8 (CCK-8) assay [59–62]. In a typical procedure, cells were seeded in 96-well microplates at a density of 5×104 cells mL−1 in 160μL of respective media containing 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS). After 24h of cell attachment, the cells were incubated with different concentrations of FSNPs-poly(IA-co-PEGMA) (20–160μgmL−1) for 12 and 24h. Then FSNPs-poly(IA-co-PEGMA) were removed and cells were washed with phosphate buffer solution (PBS) three times. 10μL of CCK-8 dye and 100μL of DMEM cell culture medium were added to each well and incubated for 2h at room temperature. Afterward, plates were analyzed using a microplate reader (VictorШ, Perkin-Elmer). Measurements of formazan dye absorbance were carried out at 450nm, with the reference wavelength at 620nm. The values were proportional to the number of live cells. The percent reduction of CCK-8 dye was compared to controls, which represented 100% CCK-8 reduction. Three replicate wells were used per microplate, and the experiment was operated for three times. Cell survival was expressed as absorbance relative to that of untreated controls. Results are presented as mean±standard deviation (SD).

HeLa cells were cultured in Dulbecco’s modified eagle medium (DMEM) supplemented with 10% heat-inactivated FBS, 2mM glutamine, 100UmL−1 penicillin, and 100μgmL−1 of streptomycin. Cell culture was controlled at 37°C in a similar humidified condition of 95% air and 5% CO2 in culture medium. Culture medium should be updated every three days for maintaining the exponential growth of the cells. Before treatment, cells were seeded in a glass bottom dish with a density of 1×105 cells per dish. On the day of treatment, the cells were incubated with FSNPs-poly(IA-co-PEGMA) at a final concentration of 40μgmL−1 for 3h at 37°C. Afterward, the cells were washed three times with PBS to remove FSNPs-poly(IA-co-PEGMA) and then fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde for 10min at room temperature. Cell images were obtained using a confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM) Zesis 710 3-channel (Zesis, Germany) with the excitation wavelength of 488nm.

The ATPHE with two amino groups was prepared according to our previous literature [58]. Its chemical structure is shown in Fig. S1 and is characterized by standard spectroscopic techniques. It has been demonstrated that ATPHE containing into polymers copolymers show AIE-active feature. Therefore FSNPs-poly(IA-co-PEGMA) will also showed AIE characteristics owing to ATPHE was encapsulated in silica nanoparticles. As compared with our previous work, the size and morphology of silica nanoparticles can be well controlled. On the other hand, the surface of silica nanoparticles can be further coated with polymers and covered with mesoporous silica shells to endow novel functionalities (e.g. water dispersibility, high specific surface areas and functional groups), making these luminescent silica nanomaterials good candidates for fabrication of multifunctional theranostic systems. In this work, ATPHE as a fluorogen was non-covalently encapsulated into silica nanoparticles via a modified Stöber method. Then these as-prepared core-shell FSNPs were subsequently functionalized with APTES to introduce amino groups, which can be utilized for immobilization of the synthetic polymers poly(IA-co-PEGMA) through ring-opening reaction between amine and anhydride groups. The synthesized FSNPs-poly(IA-co-PEGMA) with bright fluorescence, controllable particle size, excellent biocompatibility and abundant carboxyl groups are promising for biomedical applications such as cell imaging and drug delivery.

The morphology and particle size of FSNPs could be clearly observed by the TEM images (Fig. 2 ). TEM image indicates that FSNPs possess a spherical shape and uniform particle size (about 110nm). In addition, lots of obvious black spots could be clearly observed in the interior of FSNPs, which demonstrated that ATPHE had been successfully embedded into silica nanoparticles via a Stöber method. After surface modification of FSNPs-NH2 with poly(IA-co-PEGMA) via ring-opening reaction between amine and anhydride groups, the size and morphology of silica nanoparticles was not obviously changed, however, many inter-particle linkage between silica nanoparticles were observed (Fig. 2B). Furthermore, the hydrodynamic size distribution of FSNPs-poly(IA-co-PEGMA) in water was determined by DLS. The results suggested that the size distribution of FSNPs-poly(IA-co-PEGMA) is 361±8nm, with a narrow polydispersity index (PDI) of 0.005 (Fig. S2). The difference between TEM and DLS is possibly ascribed to the inter-particle linkage between silica nanoparticles. The above results implied that these silica nanoparticles were surface modification with polymer through the cross-linking reaction between amino groups of silica nanoparticles and anhydride groups of poly(IA-co-PEGMA).

Fig. 3 illustrates the FT-IR spectra of FSNPs, FSNPs-NH2 and FSNPs-poly(IA-co-PEGMA). As shown in Fig. 3, the absorption peak at 798cm−1 can be ascribed to the symmetric vibration of the SiO groups, and the asymmetric vibration of the SiO groups was located at 1095cm−1. Other characteristic absorption peak at 974cm−1 is ascribed to the asymmetric vibration of the Si-OH groups. There are three kinds of characteristic absorption bands can be clearly observed in the above three samples. In addition, the absorption peak of amino groups was appeared at 3468cm−1, evidencing the successful fabrication of FSNPs-NH2 through silane coupling reaction between APTES and FSNPs. However, as compared with FSNPs and FSNPs-NH2, the stretching vibration bands of CO groups at 1653cm−1 was appeared in the simple of FSNPs-poly(IA-co-PEGMA), indicating that these FSNPs were surface functionalized with a dense polymer coating uccessfully.

The chemical compositions of FSNPs, FSNPs-NH2 and FSNPs-poly(IA-co-PEGMA) can be further confirmed by XPS. The survey scan spectra were shown in Fig. S3. Five elements, i.e., silicon (Si), carbon (C), nitrogen (N), oxygen (O), and sulfur (S) were found in all the samples. It can also be observed that the major elements in these samples are Si, O and C. It is very interest to notice that intensive signals of C were observed in as-prepared FSNPs and FSNPs-NH2. The C should originate from the non-hydrolysis APTES and encapsulated AIE dye. Moreover, we could also clearly find that the signals of C in the sample of FSNPs-poly(IA-co-PEGMA) were obviously increased. This implied that poly(IA-co-PEGMA) polymers were introduced on FSNPs-NH2 through ring-opening reaction between the amino groups of FSNPs-NH2 and anhydride groups of poly(IA-co-PEGMA). The detailed elemental information of these samples can be further obtained from Si2p, C1s, N1s, O1s and S2p XPS spectra shown in Figs. 4 A–D and S4. As compared with the sample of FSNPs, it is obvious that the N1s signal of FSNPs-NH2 is significantly enhanced in N1s spectra, suggesting that APTES has been successfully immobilized on the surface of FSNPs through hydrolysis of APTES. In addition, it is clear to see that the peak intensity of Si2p continues to decline from the sample of FSNPs to FSNPs-poly(IA-co-PEGMA), meanwhile the peak intensity of C1s increased at first and then decreased. More importantly, there is a significant increase in O1s intensity of FSNPs-poly(IA-co-PEGMA) compared to FSNPs-NH2. These results provide favorable evidence that poly(IA-co-PEGMA) has been immobilized onto the surface of FSNPs-NH2 by ring-opening reaction. Unfortunately, the S2p spectra shown in Fig. S4 are always very weak. The possible reason is that element S of ATPHE was encapsulated in the silica core and difficult to be detected by XPS.

Furthermore, Table 1 shows the percentages of element content of FSNPs, FSNPs-NH2 and FSNPs-poly(IA-co-PEGMA) based on XPS analysis. The pure FSNPs are mainly consisted of O (51.67%), Si (25.39%) and C (21.68%), and a little N (0.78%) and S (0.48%). Since FSNPs were modified with APTES, the percentages of N are increased from 0.78% to 2.80% and the percentages of other elements such as Si, C, O and S are changed to 19.36%, 38.76%, 38.43% and 0.65%, respectively. The increase of the percentages of N and C contents indicated that APTES has been successfully conjugated to the surface of FSNPs via hydrolysis reaction of APTES. Moreover, the percentages of O are increased from 38.43% to 59.16% after FSNPs-NH2 was functionalized with poly(IA-co-PEGMA). Other element contents of Si, C, N and S are changed to 12.26%, 26.63%, 1.24% and 0.71%, respectively. Therefore, all the results suggested that poly(IA-co-PEGMA) have been coated on the surface of FSNPs-NH2 via ring-opening reaction between amino groups of FSNPs-NH2 and anhydride groups of poly(IA-co-PEGMA).

Owing to encapsulation of AIE-active dye in silica nanoparticles, FSNPs-poly(IA-co-PEGMA) exhibit unique good water dispersity and intensive fluorescence. As shown in the inset of Fig. 5 , uniform water suspension was found after FSNPs-poly(IA-co-PEGMA) deposited for over 12h (left cuvette). On the other hand, bright and uniform red fluorescence could be obviously observed when FSNPs-poly(IA-co-PEGMA) water suspension was excited by UV lamp (λ=365nm). Furthermore, the fluorescence spectra of FSNPs-poly(IA-co-PEGMA) are shown in Fig. 5. The maximum emission and excitation wavelength were centered at 595 and 495nm, respectively. More importantly, we also found the excitation peak is very broad (from 390 to 595nm). This implied that the FSNPs can be excited by the lasers with different wavelengths. Moreover, it is worth to mention that as-prepared FSNPs-poly(IA-co-PEGMA) also exhibit excellent photo-stability. As shown in Fig. S5, only very few fluorescent intensity decrease was found when FSNPs-poly(IA-co-PEGMA) were continuously irradiated by UV lamp at 365nm for 1h. Therefore, these results indicated that FSNPs-poly(IA-co-PEGMA) possess high water dispersity, unique optical properties and desirable photo-stability, suggesting their potential for biological imaging applications.

In order to further verify potential applications of FSNPs-poly(IA-co-PEGMA) in biomedical fields, the CCK-8 assay was carried out to evaluate cell viability of FSNPs-poly(IA-co-PEGMA). As shown in Fig. 6 , it is obvious that no significant decrease of cell viability values could be observed after HeLa cells were incubated with 20–160μgmL−1 of FSNPs-poly(IA-co-PEGMA) for different time. More importantly, it is noteworthy that cell viability values are still greater than 90% even the concentrations of FSNPs-poly(IA-co-PEGMA) were arrived to 160μgmL−1. The results demonstrated that FSNPs-poly(IA-co-PEGMA) have excellent biocompatibility and low toxicity towards HeLa cells, indicating the promising potential for cell imaging application.

FSNPs-poly(IA-co-PEGMA) possess many remarkable properties, which include uniform particle size, unique optical properties, excellent biocompatibility and low toxicity. These features implied FSNPs-poly(IA-co-PEGMA) are suitable for biomedical applications. Therefore, the cell uptake behavior of FSNPs-poly(IA-co-PEGMA) was evaluated by CLSM observation. As shown in Fig. 7 A, after HeLa cells incubated with 50μgmL−1 of FSNPs-poly(IA-co-PEGMA) for 3h. The cell plates were examined by CLSM after the cell plates were excited with 488nm laser and intensive fluorescence signals were detected. The size of areas with fluorescent signals is about 20–30μm. More importantly, many areas absent of fluorescence signals were surrounded by the areas with intensive fluorescent signals. Based on the above observation, we could conjecture that the areas with intensive fluorescence signals are the locations of cells while the areas in the center locations should be the positions of cell nuclei. The cell size, morphology and positions can be evidenced by the bright field image. As displayed from Fig. 7B, normal cellular morphology was maintained after cells were incubated with these luminescent nanoparticles for 3h, further demonstrating the excellent biocompatibility of FSNPs-poly(IA-co-PEGMA). Moreover, the merged image shown in Fig. 7C clearly showed that the locations of cells were exactly overlapped by the fluorescence signals. Thus, the above results clearly confirmed that FSNPs-poly(IA-co-PEGMA) can be internalized into the cells through endocytosis and distributed mainly in the cell cytoplasm. Furthermore, as-prepared FSNPs-poly(IA-co-PEGMA) are not only just suitable for cell imaging, but also possess a huge potential for many other biomedical applications. For example, a large number of carboxyl groups were generated during the procedure for immobilization of polymers on FSNPs. These carboxyl groups can be used for loading anticancer agent cisplatin and conjugating with targeting agents. It is therefore FSNPs-poly(IA-co-PEGMA) can be applied for drug delivery applications.

In summary, we have developed a facile strategy to prepare core-shell FSNPs with the AIE-active dye ATPHE as the core and PEG-containing polymers as the shell. The obtained FSNPs-poly(IA-co-PEGMA) were characterized by TEM, FT-IR, XPS and PL spectroscopy. It can draw a conclusion that FSNPs-poly(IA-co-PEGMA) have been prepared successfully. The cell viability as well as cell uptake behavior has also been investigated to evaluate their potential for biomedical applications. Results suggested that FSNPs-poly(IA-co-PEGMA) exhibited low cytotoxicity and can be effectively internalized by HeLa cells. More importantly, due to the existence of abundant carboxyl groups on the surface of FSNPs-poly(IA-co-PEGMA). The FSNPs-poly(IA-co-PEGMA) can thus be further conjugated with many other functional components and utilized for carrying the anticancer agent. It is therefore multifunctional FSNPs can thus be fabricated and show great potential for various biomedical applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the National Science Foundation of China (Nos. 51363016, 21474057, 21564006, 21561022, 21644014, 51673107), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangxi Province in China (Nos. 20161BAB203072, 20161BAB213066).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2017.01.234.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Gold nanoparticles in biomedical applications: recent advances and perspectives
PEGylation of fluoridated hydroxyapatite (FAp): Ln3+ nanorods for cell imaging
Silica-based nanocapsules: synthesis, structure control and biomedical applications
Multifunctional mesoporous silica nanocomposite nanoparticles for theranostic applications
Facile incorporation of aggregation-induced emission materials into mesoporous silica nanoparticles for intracellular imaging and cancer therapy

Size tunable fluorescent nano-graphite oxides: preparation and cell imaging applications

Fluorescence enhancement of glutaraldehyde functionalized polyaniline nanofibers in the presence of aromatic amino acids
Effect of a carbonated HAP/β-glucan composite bone substitute on healing of drilled bone voids in the proximal tibial metaphysis of rabbits
Fabrication of autofluorescent porous silica nanoparticles for redox-responsive drug release
Aptamer-conjugated, fluorescent gold nanorods as potential cancer theradiagnostic agents
Synthesis and characterization of a HAp-based biomarker with controlled drug release for breast cancer

Synthesis of different sized and porous hydroxyapatite nanorods without organic modifiers and their 5-fluorouracil release performance
Preparation and characterization of nano-sized hydroxyapatite/alginate/chitosan composite scaffolds for bone tissue engineering
Fluorescent core–shell silica nanoparticles: towards Lab on a particle architectures for nanobiotechnology
Preparation of silica nanoparticles based multifunctional therapeutic systems via one-step mussel inspired modification
Self-polymerization of dopamine and polyethyleneimine: novel fluorescent organic nanoprobes for biological imaging applications
Biocompatible polydopamine fluoresecent organic nanoparticles: facile preparation and cell imaging
Ultrafast preparation of AIE-Active fluorescent organic nanoparticles via a one-pot microwave-assisted kabachnik–fields reaction

Aggregation-induced emission: together we shine, united we soar!

Preparation of water soluble and biocompatible AIE-active fluorescent organic nanoparticles via multicomponent reaction and their biological imaging capability
